title: Infective Endocarditis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Internal Medicine, Cardiology, Infectious Disease
objectives: Identify typical vs atypical IE pathogens
Understand imaging modalities including role of PET/CT
Select appropriate empiric and pathogen-directed antibiotic therapy

vignette.text:
A 58-year-old man with history of bicuspid aortic valve presents with 3 weeks of fevers, fatigue, and night sweats. He had a dental extraction 6 weeks ago. He reports new dyspnea on exertion.

Past Medical History: Bicuspid aortic valve, hypertension

Medications: Lisinopril 10mg daily

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.cardiac: III/VI systolic ejection murmur at RUSB radiating to carotids, NEW III/VI early diastolic murmur at left sternal border
physicalExam.extremities: Splinter hemorrhages in nail beds, Janeway lesions on palms

questions.question: What are the 2023 Duke-ISCVID diagnostic criteria?

options.text: The 2023 criteria are identical to 2000 modified Duke
Blood cultures from different days are required
Only echocardiography can satisfy major criteria

2023 DUKE-ISCVID CRITERIA: DEFINITE IE: Pathologic criteria (microorganisms or active endocarditis on histology) OR Clinical criteria (2 major, OR 1 major + 3 minor, OR 5 minor); MAJOR CRITERIA: (1) MICROBIOLOGY: Typical organisms from ≥2 blood cultures (S. aureus, Viridans strep, S. gallolyticus, HACEK, E. faecalis); OR Atypical organisms from ≥3 cultures; OR Positive PCR/serology for C. burnetii, Bartonella, T. whipplei; (2) IMAGING: Echo: vegetation, abscess, pseudoaneurysm, perforation, dehiscence; Cardiac CT with same findings; [18F]FDG PET/CT (prosthetic valves/devices); Intraoperative visualization (NEW in 2023); MINOR: Predisposition, fever ≥38°C, vascular phenomena, immunologic phenomena, supportive microbiology

questions.explanation: 2023 Duke-ISCVID Diagnostic Criteria (Major Update): KEY CHANGES FROM 2000 MODIFIED DUKE: Removed timing requirements for blood cultures. Added PET/CT and cardiac CT as imaging criteria. Added intraoperative inspection as major criterion. Expanded list of typical organisms. Added molecular diagnostics (PCR, sequencing). DEFINITE IE: Pathologic criteria: Microorganisms identified OR Active endocarditis on histology from vegetation/abscess/intracardiac tissue. Clinical criteria: 2 Major, OR 1 Major + 3 Minor, OR 5 Minor. MAJOR CRITERIA: 1. MICROBIOLOGIC: Typical organisms from ≥2 separate blood culture sets: S. aureus, Viridans streptococci, S. gallolyticus (bovis), HACEK organisms, E. faecalis, S. lugdunensis. Atypical organisms from ≥3 separate sets. Coxiella burnetii (single culture OR anti-phase I IgG >1:800). PCR positive for C. burnetii, Bartonella, T. whipplei. Bartonella serology (IgG ≥1:800). 2. IMAGING: Echocardiography: vegetation, abscess, pseudoaneurysm, perforation, new dehiscence. Cardiac CT: same findings. [18F]FDG PET/CT: abnormal activity around prosthetic valve/device. Intraoperative inspection: vegetation, abscess, perforation (NEW). MINOR CRITERIA: Predisposing condition (valve disease, IVDU, prosthetic valve, prior IE). Fever ≥38°C. Vascular phenomena (emboli, Janeway lesions, mycotic aneurysm). Immunologic phenomena (Osler nodes, Roth spots, GN, RF). Supportive microbiology not meeting major criteria.

sources.text: StatPearls: Infective Endocarditis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has fever, predisposing valve disease, vascular phenomena (Janeway lesions, splinter hemorrhages), and a new murmur.
questions.question: How does this patient meet Duke criteria?

options.text: Janeway lesions are immunologic not vascular phenomena
Bicuspid aortic valve is not a predisposing condition

APPLYING CRITERIA TO THIS PATIENT: LIKELY MAJOR: Echo pending (expect vegetation on bicuspid valve); Blood cultures pending (expect typical organism); DEFINITE MINOR: Predisposition: Bicuspid aortic valve (structural heart disease); Fever: 101.8°F (38.8°C) ≥38°C; Vascular phenomena: Janeway lesions + splinter hemorrhages; CURRENT STATUS: At minimum 3 minor criteria already met; If echo shows vegetation OR blood cultures positive with typical organism → 1 Major + 3 Minor = DEFINITE IE; New regurgitant murmur (diastolic) strongly suggests valve destruction; CLINICAL PEARL: Don't need to wait for criteria - treat empirically if high suspicion while awaiting workup results
Patient cannot have IE without positive blood cultures

questions.explanation: Applying Duke Criteria to This Patient: MINOR CRITERIA ALREADY MET: 1. PREDISPOSITION: Bicuspid aortic valve (structural valve abnormality). Dental procedure (recent portal of entry). 2. FEVER: 101.8°F (38.8°C) meets ≥38°C threshold. 3. VASCULAR PHENOMENA: Janeway lesions (painless erythematous lesions on palms/soles). Splinter hemorrhages (though less specific). PENDING WORKUP: Blood cultures (likely positive for typical organism given presentation). Echocardiography (likely to show vegetation). NEW REGURGITANT MURMUR: New diastolic murmur is highly concerning for valve destruction. Suggests aortic regurgitation from vegetation/perforation. While technically not a Duke criterion itself, strongly supports diagnosis. CLASSIFICATION: Currently: 3 Minor criteria = POSSIBLE IE. If blood cultures positive with typical organism: 1 Major (microbiology) + 3 Minor = DEFINITE IE. If echo shows vegetation: 2 Major = DEFINITE IE. CLINICAL APPROACH: High clinical suspicion warrants empiric treatment while awaiting results. Don't delay treatment waiting for criteria to be met. Obtain blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics when possible.

sources.text: StatPearls: Infective Endocarditis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What are the typical pathogens causing native valve endocarditis?

options.text: Viridans streptococci are the most common cause overall
Enterococcus is rare in native valve IE

NATIVE VALVE ENDOCARDITIS PATHOGENS: TYPICAL (2023 Duke-ISCVID): S. aureus: Most common overall (30-40%); Healthcare-associated, IVDU; Aggressive, high mortality; Viridans streptococci: Classic IE organism; Dental source; Subacute presentation; S. gallolyticus (bovis): Associated with colon cancer - GET COLONOSCOPY; Enterococcus faecalis: Older patients, GI/GU procedures; May require synergistic therapy; HACEK organisms: Fastidious gram-negatives; May need extended incubation; S. lugdunensis: CoNS but behaves like S. aureus; ATYPICAL (need ≥3 cultures): Other streptococci; CoNS (non-lugdunensis); Gram-negatives; CULTURE-NEGATIVE (5-10%): Prior antibiotics; Fastidious organisms (Bartonella, Coxiella, Tropheryma)
Coagulase-negative staph always indicates contamination

questions.explanation: Native Valve Endocarditis Microbiology: TYPICAL ORGANISMS (2023 Duke-ISCVID Major Criterion - need only 2 positive cultures): STAPHYLOCOCCUS AUREUS: Most common cause overall (30-40%). Healthcare-associated and community-acquired. IVDU-associated (often right-sided). Aggressive course, high mortality (25-40%). Viridans Group Streptococci: Classic endocarditis pathogen. Dental/oral source. Subacute presentation. Generally better prognosis. Streptococcus gallolyticus (formerly S. bovis): GI tract source. STRONGLY associated with colon cancer/polyps. MUST get colonoscopy! Enterococcus faecalis: Older patients. GI/GU tract source. May need synergistic aminoglycoside therapy. E. faecium typically requires prosthetic material to be typical. HACEK Organisms: Haemophilus, Aggregatibacter, Cardiobacterium, Eikenella, Kingella. Fastidious gram-negatives. Oral flora, large vegetations. May need extended incubation. Staphylococcus lugdunensis: CoNS but behaves aggressively like S. aureus. Considered typical even though CoNS. ATYPICAL ORGANISMS (need ≥3 positive cultures): Other streptococci (S. pneumoniae, S. pyogenes). Non-lugdunensis CoNS. Gram-negative bacilli (Pseudomonas, Enterobacteriaceae). CULTURE-NEGATIVE IE (5-10%): Prior antibiotic therapy (most common cause). Fastidious organisms: Bartonella, Coxiella burnetii, Tropheryma whipplei. Consider molecular testing/serology.

sources.text: StatPearls: Infective Endocarditis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: How does microbiology differ in prosthetic valve endocarditis?

options.text: Rifampin is not needed for prosthetic valve IE

PROSTHETIC VALVE ENDOCARDITIS (PVE): EARLY PVE (<60 days post-op): Healthcare-associated infection; S. aureus: Most common; Coagulase-negative staphylococci (especially S. epidermidis); Gram-negatives (including Pseudomonas); Fungi (Candida, Aspergillus); Corynebacterium; INTERMEDIATE PVE (60-365 days): Mix of early and late organisms; LATE PVE (>1 year): Similar to native valve IE; Viridans streptococci; Enterococcus; S. aureus; 2023 UPDATE - Additional 'typical' organisms for prosthetic material: CoNS (all species); Corynebacterium striatum, C. jeikeium; Serratia marcescens; Pseudomonas aeruginosa; Cutibacterium acnes; KEY: All PVE requires RIFAMPIN in regimen (biofilm penetration)
Early and late PVE have identical microbiology
Fungi are rare in prosthetic valve IE

questions.explanation: Prosthetic Valve Endocarditis Microbiology: EARLY PVE (within 60 days of surgery): Perioperative/healthcare-associated acquisition. S. aureus (most common). Coagulase-negative staphylococci (S. epidermidis). Gram-negative bacilli (including Pseudomonas). Fungi (Candida, Aspergillus). Corynebacterium species. Higher mortality than late PVE. INTERMEDIATE PVE (60-365 days): Transitional period. Mix of healthcare-associated and community organisms. LATE PVE (>1 year after surgery): Community-acquired. Similar organisms to native valve IE. Viridans streptococci. Enterococcus. S. aureus. S. gallolyticus. 2023 DUKE-ISCVID UPDATE - Organisms Typical ONLY WITH PROSTHETIC MATERIAL: All coagulase-negative staphylococci (not just S. lugdunensis). Corynebacterium striatum. Corynebacterium jeikeium. Serratia marcescens. Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Cutibacterium acnes (formerly Propionibacterium). These require only 2 positive cultures when prosthetic material present. CRITICAL TREATMENT PRINCIPLE: ALL prosthetic valve IE requires RIFAMPIN in regimen. Rifampin penetrates biofilm on prosthetic material. Without rifampin, treatment failure rates high. Add rifampin after blood cultures clear (prevents resistance development). HIGHER MORTALITY: PVE has higher mortality than NVE (20-40% vs 15-25%). Often requires surgical intervention.

sources.text: StatPearls: Infective Endocarditis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the role of echocardiography in IE?

options.text: ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY IN IE: TTE (Transthoracic): First-line imaging; Sensitivity 50-70% for NVE; Adequate if high-quality and clearly positive; TEE (Transesophageal): Sensitivity 90-100%; INDICATED when: TTE non-diagnostic or negative with high suspicion; Prosthetic valve (TTE sensitivity only 40-50%); Suspected complications (abscess, perforation); S. aureus bacteremia; Poor TTE windows; ECHO FINDINGS (Major Criteria): Vegetation (oscillating mass on valve); Abscess (thickened/echo-lucent perivalvular area); Pseudoaneurysm; Valvular perforation; New dehiscence of prosthetic valve; NEW IMAGING IN 2023: Cardiac CT (especially for abscess/pseudoaneurysm); [18F]FDG PET/CT for prosthetic valve/device IE
TEE should be avoided due to procedural risks
TTE is adequate for all suspected IE cases
Negative TTE rules out endocarditis

questions.explanation: Echocardiography in IE: TRANSTHORACIC ECHO (TTE): First-line imaging in suspected IE. Sensitivity: 50-70% for native valve, 40-50% for prosthetic valve. Specificity: >90%. Use when: Initial evaluation. Low clinical suspicion. Good acoustic windows. TRANSESOPHAGEAL ECHO (TEE): Sensitivity: 90-100%. INDICATIONS: TTE negative or non-diagnostic with high clinical suspicion. Prosthetic valve (TTE has poor sensitivity). S. aureus bacteremia (high IE risk). Suspected complications (abscess, fistula). Poor TTE windows (obesity, COPD). Prior to surgery. ECHOCARDIOGRAPHIC FINDINGS (2023 Duke-ISCVID Major Criteria): Vegetation: Oscillating mass attached to valve/endocardium, or in path of regurgitant jet, or on implanted material. Abscess: Thickened, echo-lucent perivalvular region. Pseudoaneurysm: Pulsatile perivalvular echo-free space. Perforation: Breach in valve tissue. New dehiscence: Partial separation of prosthetic valve. NEW IMAGING MODALITIES (2023 Update): CARDIAC CT: Good for detecting abscess, pseudoaneurysm. Useful when TEE suboptimal. [18F]FDG PET/CT: For prosthetic valve and device IE. Detects inflammation around prosthetic material. Helps identify peripheral embolic events. Can identify primary and secondary foci. TIMING: Initial echo within 48 hours of suspected IE. Repeat in 5-7 days if initially negative but suspicion remains. Pre-operative TEE if surgery planned.

sources.text: StatPearls: Infective Endocarditis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Blood cultures are drawn and you need to start empiric therapy.
questions.question: What is the empiric antibiotic regimen for native valve IE?

options.text: Vancomycin alone is adequate empiric therapy

EMPIRIC NATIVE VALVE IE: COMMUNITY-ACQUIRED: Ampicillin 2g IV q4h + Oxacillin/Nafcillin 2g IV q4h + Gentamicin 3mg/kg/day; Covers: Streptococci, MSSA, Enterococcus; If PENICILLIN ALLERGY or MRSA risk: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h (trough 15-20) + Gentamicin; HEALTHCARE-ASSOCIATED or IVDU: Vancomycin (for MRSA coverage) + Gentamicin ± Cefepime or antipseudomonal (if gram-negative risk); CRITICALLY ILL: Vancomycin + Gentamicin + Oxacillin (or substitute broader gram-negative coverage); ADJUST based on: Blood culture results; Susceptibility data; Always narrow therapy when organism identified
Gentamicin is never used in empiric regimens
Oral antibiotics can be used for empiric therapy

questions.explanation: Empiric Antibiotic Therapy for Native Valve IE: COMMUNITY-ACQUIRED NVE: Ampicillin 2g IV q4h (covers streptococci, enterococci) PLUS Oxacillin or Nafcillin 2g IV q4h (covers MSSA) PLUS Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day IV (synergy for streptococci/enterococci). Alternative if penicillin allergy: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h (target trough 15-20 µg/mL) PLUS Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day. HEALTHCARE-ASSOCIATED or IVDU: Vancomycin (MRSA coverage) PLUS Gentamicin ± Cefepime or antipseudomonal beta-lactam (gram-negative coverage if indicated). CRITICALLY ILL PATIENTS: Broader coverage initially. Vancomycin + Gentamicin + Oxacillin/Nafcillin (or broader based on risk factors). GENERAL PRINCIPLES: OBTAIN BLOOD CULTURES BEFORE ANTIBIOTICS. At least 3 sets from different sites. Gentamicin provides synergy, not standalone coverage. Duration of gentamicin usually limited (2 weeks or less) due to nephrotoxicity. Adjust therapy once culture and susceptibility results available. ALWAYS narrow spectrum when organism identified. DURATION: Full therapy is typically 4-6 weeks IV. Oral completion protocols emerging for selected patients (after initial IV phase).

questions.stem: You're treating a patient with prosthetic valve IE.
questions.question: How does empiric therapy differ for prosthetic valve IE?

options.text: PROSTHETIC VALVE IE EMPIRIC THERAPY: BROADER COVERAGE NEEDED: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h (trough 15-20); + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day IV; + Rifampin 300-450mg PO q8h (ADD AFTER CULTURES CLEAR); If early PVE or healthcare-associated: Add Cefepime 2g IV q8h (gram-negative/Pseudomonas); KEY DIFFERENCES FROM NVE: ALWAYS include MRSA coverage (vancomycin); Gentamicin essential for synergy; RIFAMPIN CRITICAL: Penetrates biofilm on prosthetic material; Add once blood cultures negative (prevents resistance); Continue throughout therapy; Longer duration: Minimum 6 weeks; Consider antifungal coverage if early PVE
Same regimen as native valve IE
4 weeks of therapy is sufficient for PVE
Rifampin should be started immediately

questions.explanation: Empiric Therapy for Prosthetic Valve IE: STANDARD EMPIRIC REGIMEN: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h (target trough 15-20 µg/mL). Covers MRSA, CoNS, enterococci. PLUS Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day IV. Synergy for staphylococci and enterococci. PLUS Rifampin 300-450 mg PO/IV q8h. CRITICAL for prosthetic material. ADD ONLY AFTER BLOOD CULTURES CLEAR. Prevents rifampin resistance development. EARLY PVE OR HEALTHCARE-ASSOCIATED: Add Cefepime 2g IV q8h or other antipseudomonal. Covers Pseudomonas, Serratia, other gram-negatives. Consider antifungal coverage if high risk. KEY PRINCIPLES FOR PVE: RIFAMPIN IS ESSENTIAL: Only agent that reliably penetrates prosthetic biofilm. Without it, treatment failure rates high. Must add after bacteremia clears (resistance prevention). Continue throughout therapy. DURATION LONGER: Minimum 6 WEEKS from first negative blood culture or surgery. Some organisms may require longer. HIGHER TREATMENT FAILURE RATE: Surgical intervention often required. Lower threshold for early surgery than NVE. NARROW THERAPY: Once organism identified, adjust regimen. But always maintain rifampin for prosthetic material.

sources.text: StatPearls: Infective Endocarditis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Blood cultures grow Viridans streptococcus (penicillin MIC 0.12 µg/mL).
questions.question: What is the treatment for streptococcal native valve IE?

options.text: Oral therapy can be used throughout
Gentamicin is never needed for streptococcal IE

STREPTOCOCCAL NVE TREATMENT: PENICILLIN-SUSCEPTIBLE (MIC ≤0.12 µg/mL): Option 1: Penicillin G 12-18 million units/day IV continuously or divided q4h × 4 weeks; Option 2: Ceftriaxone 2g IV/IM daily × 4 weeks (convenient once-daily); Option 3: Penicillin or Ceftriaxone × 2 weeks + Gentamicin × 2 weeks (shorter course with synergy); RELATIVELY RESISTANT (MIC 0.12-0.5 µg/mL): Penicillin G 24 million units/day × 4 weeks + Gentamicin × 2 weeks; Or Ceftriaxone 2g daily × 4 weeks + Gentamicin × 2 weeks; PENICILLIN ALLERGY: Vancomycin 30 mg/kg/day IV in 2 divided doses × 4 weeks; S. GALLOLYTICUS: Same regimens BUT: Get colonoscopy (colon cancer association)
2 weeks of therapy is sufficient for all streptococcal IE

questions.explanation: Streptococcal Native Valve IE Treatment: PENICILLIN-SUSCEPTIBLE (MIC ≤0.12 µg/mL): OPTION 1 (4-week monotherapy): Penicillin G 12-18 million units/day IV continuously or divided q4h × 4 weeks. OR Ceftriaxone 2g IV/IM once daily × 4 weeks (convenient). OPTION 2 (2-week combination - for uncomplicated IE): Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone (as above) × 2 weeks PLUS Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day IV × 2 weeks. Synergistic combination allows shorter course. NOT for patients with: Extracardiac infection, Renal insufficiency, Prosthetic valve, Complicated IE. RELATIVELY RESISTANT (MIC >0.12 to ≤0.5 µg/mL): Penicillin G 24 million units/day IV × 4 weeks PLUS Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day × 2 weeks. OR Ceftriaxone 2g daily × 4 weeks PLUS Gentamicin × 2 weeks. PENICILLIN ALLERGY: Vancomycin 30 mg/kg/day IV divided q8-12h × 4 weeks. Target trough 10-15 µg/mL. S. GALLOLYTICUS (BOVIS): Same treatment regimens. CRITICAL: Must obtain COLONOSCOPY. Strong association with colon cancer and polyps. Screen regardless of GI symptoms. PROSTHETIC VALVE STREPTOCOCCAL IE: 6 weeks total duration. Consider adding rifampin.

sources.text: StatPearls: Infective Endocarditis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the treatment for staphylococcal native valve IE?

options.text: Vancomycin is preferred over nafcillin for MSSA

STAPHYLOCOCCAL NVE TREATMENT: MSSA: Nafcillin or Oxacillin 2g IV q4h × 6 weeks; Alternative: Cefazolin 2g IV q8h × 6 weeks; Gentamicin NOT recommended (no benefit, more nephrotoxicity); MRSA: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h × 6 weeks (trough 15-20); OR Daptomycin 8-10 mg/kg IV daily × 6 weeks (if vancomycin intolerant); RIGHT-SIDED (Tricuspid) MSSA in IVDU: Nafcillin/Oxacillin × 2 weeks (short course); ONLY if: Uncomplicated, no extracardiac infection, responds rapidly, no left-sided involvement; PROSTHETIC VALVE STAPH: Nafcillin/Oxacillin (MSSA) or Vancomycin (MRSA) + Rifampin 300mg q8h + Gentamicin × 2 weeks; Then Nafcillin/Vancomycin + Rifampin to complete ≥6 weeks
Gentamicin should be added for all S. aureus IE
2 weeks is standard for all S. aureus IE

questions.explanation: Staphylococcal Native Valve IE Treatment: MSSA NATIVE VALVE: PREFERRED: Nafcillin or Oxacillin 2g IV q4h × 6 weeks. ALTERNATIVE: Cefazolin 2g IV q8h × 6 weeks (if penicillin allergy without anaphylaxis). IMPORTANT: Gentamicin NOT routinely recommended. Studies show no clinical benefit. Increased nephrotoxicity. MRSA NATIVE VALVE: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h × 6 weeks. Target trough 15-20 µg/mL. OR Daptomycin 8-10 mg/kg IV daily × 6 weeks. Use if vancomycin failure/intolerance. Higher doses in IE (not 4-6 mg/kg). RIGHT-SIDED (TRICUSPID) IE IN IVDU: MSSA only: 2-week course may be adequate. Nafcillin/Oxacillin × 2 weeks. STRICT CRITERIA: Uncomplicated (no metastatic infection). No left-sided involvement. Responds within 72 hours. No renal failure. No immunocompromise. PROSTHETIC VALVE STAPHYLOCOCCAL IE: MSSA: Nafcillin/Oxacillin × ≥6 weeks PLUS Rifampin 300-450 mg q8h × ≥6 weeks PLUS Gentamicin × first 2 weeks. MRSA: Vancomycin × ≥6 weeks PLUS Rifampin × ≥6 weeks PLUS Gentamicin × first 2 weeks. KEY: Add rifampin AFTER blood cultures clear.

questions.stem: The patient develops worsening heart failure.
questions.question: What are the indications for surgery in IE?

options.text: SURGICAL INDICATIONS IN IE (AHA 2015): HEART FAILURE (most common indication): Valve regurgitation causing refractory heart failure; Severe valve dysfunction with pulmonary edema/cardiogenic shock; URGENT/EMERGENT surgery indicated; UNCONTROLLED INFECTION: Persistent bacteremia >5-7 days despite appropriate antibiotics; Perivalvular abscess or extension; Enlarging vegetation on therapy; Infection with resistant organisms (fungi, Pseudomonas, Brucella); PREVENTION OF EMBOLISM: Large vegetations (>10mm) with embolic events; Very large vegetations (>15mm) even without prior emboli; Mobile vegetations on anterior mitral leaflet; PROSTHETIC VALVE: Dehiscence; Persistent bacteremia; Fungal etiology; Relapsing infection
Surgery is rarely indicated in IE
All patients with vegetations need surgery
Heart failure can always be managed medically

questions.explanation: Surgical Indications in IE: HEART FAILURE (Most Common Indication - ~50% of surgeries): Severe acute aortic or mitral regurgitation causing heart failure. Pulmonary edema or cardiogenic shock. Valve obstruction. TIMING: URGENT (within days) or EMERGENT (within 24 hours). Don't delay for antibiotic 'course'. UNCONTROLLED INFECTION: Persistent bacteremia (>5-7 days) despite appropriate antibiotics and no other source. Perivalvular extension: Abscess, Pseudoaneurysm, Fistula, Heart block (suggests septal abscess). Enlarging vegetation despite therapy. Infection with difficult-to-treat organisms: Fungi (Candida, Aspergillus), Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Brucella, Coxiella (Q fever). PREVENTION OF SYSTEMIC EMBOLIZATION: Large vegetations (>10mm) with prior embolic event. Very large vegetations (>15mm) especially anterior mitral leaflet. Mobile vegetations despite therapy. Must weigh against surgical risk and timing relative to recent stroke. PROSTHETIC VALVE SPECIFIC: Valve dehiscence or instability. Persistent bacteremia. Relapsing infection after appropriate therapy. Fungal IE (almost always requires surgery). TIMING CONSIDERATIONS: Recent stroke (risk of hemorrhagic conversion). Large CNS infarct: May delay 2-4 weeks if possible. Small or absent CNS lesion: Earlier surgery safer. Hemorrhagic stroke: Delay at least 4 weeks.

sources.text: StatPearls: Infective Endocarditis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient develops sudden right-sided weakness.
questions.question: What are the major complications of IE?

options.text: Renal involvement is always drug-related
Embolic complications are rare in IE
Heart block is not associated with IE

IE COMPLICATIONS: EMBOLIC (20-50%): Stroke (most common, devastating); Splenic infarcts/abscess; Renal infarcts; Pulmonary emboli (right-sided IE); Mycotic aneurysms; CARDIAC: Heart failure (valve destruction); Perivalvular abscess; Heart block (septal abscess - AV node); Pericarditis; Myocardial infarction (coronary embolism); NEUROLOGIC: Ischemic stroke; Hemorrhagic stroke (mycotic aneurysm rupture); Brain abscess; Meningitis; RENAL: Glomerulonephritis (immune complex); Renal infarcts; Drug-induced nephrotoxicity; SPLENIC: Infarcts; Abscess (may need splenectomy); For this patient: Stroke likely from septic embolus - urgent imaging and neuro consult

questions.explanation: Complications of Infective Endocarditis: EMBOLIC COMPLICATIONS (20-50% of patients): CNS embolism: Most common and devastating. Ischemic stroke. Mycotic aneurysm (can rupture causing hemorrhage). Brain abscess. Meningitis. Splenic: Infarcts (left upper quadrant pain). Splenic abscess (may require splenectomy). Renal: Infarcts (flank pain, hematuria). Pulmonary: Right-sided IE → septic pulmonary emboli. Coronary: Rare but can cause MI. Peripheral: Mycotic aneurysms. CARDIAC COMPLICATIONS: Heart failure: Most common indication for surgery. Acute valve destruction/regurgitation. Perivalvular abscess: Extension from valve infection. Can cause fistula, pseudoaneurysm. Heart block: New conduction abnormality suggests septal abscess. AV node involvement. Requires urgent evaluation for surgery. Pericarditis: Extension of infection or immune-mediated. NEUROLOGIC: 25-40% have CNS involvement. Stroke (ischemic > hemorrhagic). Mycotic aneurysms: May be asymptomatic. Can rupture causing hemorrhage. Consider screening if CNS symptoms. RENAL: Immune complex glomerulonephritis. Embolic infarcts. Antibiotic nephrotoxicity (aminoglycosides, vancomycin). THIS PATIENT: New right-sided weakness = probable embolic stroke. IMMEDIATE: CT head (rule out hemorrhage), then MRI. Neurology consultation. May impact surgical timing. Consider anticoagulation risks (hemorrhagic conversion).

sources.text: StatPearls: Infective Endocarditis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Blood cultures grew Viridans streptococcus. TEE confirms 12mm vegetation on bicuspid aortic valve with moderate-severe aortic regurgitation.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Treat medically for full 4 weeks before considering surgery

Complete approach: (1) DIAGNOSIS CONFIRMED: 2 Major criteria = Definite IE (positive cultures + vegetation); (2) ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY: Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone × 4 weeks (penicillin-susceptible strep); Check MIC to guide regimen; (3) SURGICAL EVALUATION URGENT: Large vegetation (>10mm) with embolic risk; New aortic regurgitation causing symptoms; Widened pulse pressure (128/58) suggests significant AR; Dyspnea on exertion = heart failure symptoms; (4) MONITORING: Daily exam for heart failure progression; Repeat echo if clinical change; Serial blood cultures (should clear in 48-72h); (5) COMPLICATIONS: Watch for: emboli, heart block, worsening HF; (6) SECONDARY PREVENTION: Dental evaluation/treatment; Antibiotic prophylaxis for future procedures
No need for surgical evaluation with streptococcal IE
Broad-spectrum antibiotics should continue throughout

questions.explanation: Complete Management - This Patient: STEP 1 - CONFIRM DIAGNOSIS: 2 Major criteria = DEFINITE IE. Blood cultures: Viridans streptococcus (typical organism). Imaging: 12mm vegetation on TEE. Minor criteria also present (predisposition, fever, vascular phenomena). STEP 2 - ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY: Pathogen-directed based on MIC: If MIC ≤0.12: Penicillin G 12-18 million units/day or Ceftriaxone 2g daily × 4 weeks. If MIC >0.12 to ≤0.5: Add gentamicin × 2 weeks. Duration: 4 weeks for uncomplicated NVE. STEP 3 - URGENT SURGICAL EVALUATION: Multiple indications present: Large vegetation (12mm > 10mm threshold). New significant aortic regurgitation. Heart failure symptoms (dyspnea on exertion). Wide pulse pressure (128/58 = 70 mmHg) indicates significant AR. Even with streptococcal etiology (good prognosis organism), structural indications for surgery. STEP 4 - MONITORING: Serial examinations for heart failure. Repeat blood cultures at 48-72 hours (should be negative). Daily cardiac exam (new murmurs, signs of HF). ECG monitoring (watch for heart block if perivalvular extension). Repeat echo if clinical change. STEP 5 - WATCH FOR COMPLICATIONS: Embolic events (neuro exam). Conduction abnormalities. Worsening heart failure. Perivalvular extension. STEP 6 - SECONDARY PREVENTION: Complete dental evaluation and treatment. Future antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures. Patient education on symptoms of recurrence.

sources.text: StatPearls: Infective Endocarditis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
